reference: GATE CS — Discrete Mathematics / Graph Theory

map.condition: Count perfect matchings in K_n (n even)
Check if a bipartite graph has a perfect matching
Find maximum matching size in bipartite graph
Check if matching M is maximum

map.use: \text{Every } k\text{-regular bipartite graph has a perfect matching}

formulas.name: Matching (Definition)
formulas.latex: M \subseteq E(G) \text{ such that no two edges in } M \text{ share a vertex}
formulas.plain: M is a set of edges with no two edges sharing a vertex
formulas.whenToUse: When identifying a valid matching in any graph

terms.means: matching — a set of pairwise non-adjacent edges
edge set of graph G

formulas.trap: A matching is a set of edges, not vertices; two edges sharing even one endpoint disqualify the set.

formulas.name: Maximum Matching
formulas.latex: \nu(G) = \max\{|M| : M \text{ is a matching in } G\}
formulas.plain: nu(G) = largest number of edges in any matching of G
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the greatest number of edges that can be simultaneously matched

terms.means: matching number (size of maximum matching)

formulas.trap: Maximum matching is not the same as maximal matching; a maximal matching may be much smaller than a maximum matching.

formulas.name: Perfect Matching
formulas.latex: M \text{ is perfect} \iff |M| = \frac{|V(G)|}{2} \text{ (every vertex is matched)}
formulas.plain: A matching that saturates all vertices; |M| = n/2
formulas.whenToUse: When every vertex must be matched; only possible when |V| is even

terms.means: number of vertices (must be even for perfect matching to exist)

formulas.trap: A graph with an odd number of vertices can never have a perfect matching.

formulas.name: Maximal Matching
formulas.latex: M \text{ maximal} \iff \nexists e \in E \setminus M \text{ s.t. } M \cup \{e\} \text{ is a matching}
formulas.plain: A matching where no additional edge can be added without violating the matching property
formulas.whenToUse: When checking if a matching cannot be extended further (not the same as maximum)

terms.means: an edge not in M

formulas.trap: Every maximum matching is maximal, but a maximal matching need not be maximum.

formulas.name: Perfect Matchings in K_n (Double Factorial)
formulas.latex: M(K_n) = (n-1)!! = (n-1)(n-3)(n-5)\cdots 3 \cdot 1, \quad n \text{ even}
formulas.plain: Number of perfect matchings in K_n = (n-1)!! = (n-1)(n-3)...3*1  (n even)
formulas.whenToUse: Counting perfect matchings in a complete graph on n (even) vertices

terms.means: number of vertices (must be even)
double factorial: product of odd numbers from 1 to n-1

formulas.trap: Apply only when n is even. For K_6: (6-1)!! = 5*3*1 = 15, not 6! or C(6,2).

formulas.name: Perfect Matchings in K_n (Combinatorial Form)
formulas.whenToUse: Alternative combinatorial formula when deriving the count from first principles

terms.means: total ways to arrange n vertices
divide by 2 for each pair (order in a pair does not matter)
divide by (n/2)! since order of pairs does not matter

formulas.trap: Both formulas give the same result; for K_6: 6!/(2^3 * 3!) = 720/48 = 15.

formulas.name: Perfect Matching Counts — Key Values
formulas.whenToUse: Quick recall for small complete graphs in GATE MCQs

terms.means: complete graph on n vertices

formulas.trap: K_6 gives 15, not 6 (which counts edges) or 6! (total permutations).

formulas.name: Perfect Matchings in K_{n,n}
formulas.plain: Number of perfect matchings in complete bipartite graph K_{n,n} = n!
formulas.whenToUse: Counting perfect matchings in a complete bipartite graph with equal-sized parts

terms.means: number of vertices in each partition
each of n! bijections from left to right part is a perfect matching

formulas.trap: K_{n,n} perfect matchings = n!, not (2n-1)!!; do not confuse with K_{2n}.

formulas.name: Hall's Marriage Theorem
formulas.latex: G=(A \cup B,\,E) \text{ has a matching saturating } A \iff \forall S\subseteq A:\;|N(S)|\geq|S|
formulas.plain: Bipartite G has an A-saturating matching iff for every S subset of A, |N(S)| >= |S|
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying existence of a perfect (or A-saturating) matching in a bipartite graph

terms.means: neighborhood of S — vertices in B adjacent to at least one vertex of S
two disjoint vertex partitions of the bipartite graph

formulas.trap: Hall's condition must hold for ALL subsets S of A, not just individual vertices.

formulas.name: Hall's Defect Version
formulas.latex: \nu(G) = |A| - \max_{S\subseteq A}(|S| - |N(S)|)
formulas.plain: Maximum matching size = |A| - max deficiency over all subsets S of A
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the exact size of the maximum matching when Hall's condition fails

terms.means: deficiency of set S
maximum matching number

formulas.trap: The defect formula generalises Hall's theorem; when max deficiency = 0 we recover Hall's condition.

formulas.name: König's Theorem
formulas.latex: \text{In a bipartite graph: } \nu(G) = \tau(G)
formulas.plain: In a bipartite graph, maximum matching size = minimum vertex cover size
formulas.whenToUse: Relating maximum matching to minimum vertex cover in bipartite graphs

terms.means: size of maximum matching
size of minimum vertex cover

formulas.trap: König's theorem holds only for bipartite graphs; it fails for general graphs (e.g., K_3).

formulas.name: Gallai's Identities
formulas.latex: \nu(G) + \rho(G) = n, \quad \tau(G) + \alpha(G) = n
formulas.plain: matching number + edge cover number = n;  vertex cover + independence number = n
formulas.whenToUse: Converting between matching, covering, and independence numbers

terms.means: maximum matching number
minimum edge cover (valid only if no isolated vertices)
minimum vertex cover
maximum independent set size
number of vertices

formulas.trap: Edge cover is undefined when isolated vertices exist (an isolated vertex cannot be covered by any edge).

formulas.name: Weak Duality: Matching vs Vertex Cover
formulas.latex: \nu(G) \leq \tau(G) \text{ for any graph } G
formulas.plain: Maximum matching <= minimum vertex cover in any graph
formulas.whenToUse: Lower-bounding vertex cover or upper-bounding matching without König's theorem

terms.means: maximum matching (lower bound on vertex cover)
minimum vertex cover (upper bound on matching)

formulas.trap: Equality holds only in bipartite graphs (König); for general graphs the gap can be positive.

formulas.name: Berge's Theorem
formulas.latex: M \text{ is maximum} \iff G \text{ has no } M\text{-augmenting path}
formulas.plain: A matching M is maximum iff there is no augmenting path with respect to M
formulas.whenToUse: Proving or disproving that a given matching is maximum

terms.means: a path that starts and ends at unmatched vertices and alternates between non-matching and matching edges

formulas.trap: An augmenting path must start AND end at unmatched vertices; alternating paths from matched vertices are not augmenting.

formulas.name: Augmenting Path Definition
formulas.latex: P: u_0 e_1 u_1 e_2 \cdots u_k,\; u_0,u_k \notin V(M),\; e_i \notin M \text{ (odd)},\; e_i \in M \text{ (even)}
formulas.plain: Path alternating between unmatched and matched edges, starting and ending at free vertices
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying paths that can be used to increase matching size by 1

terms.means: free (unmatched) endpoints of the augmenting path
edges alternating: not in M, in M, not in M, ...

formulas.trap: Symmetric difference M ⊕ P (toggling edges on the path) gives a matching of size |M|+1.

formulas.name: Regular Bipartite Graph Has Perfect Matching
formulas.latex: G \text{ is } k\text{-regular bipartite } (k\geq 1) \Rightarrow G \text{ has a perfect matching}
formulas.plain: Every k-regular bipartite graph (k >= 1) has a perfect matching
formulas.whenToUse: When the graph is bipartite and every vertex has the same degree

terms.means: every vertex has degree exactly k

formulas.trap: This requires bipartiteness; a k-regular non-bipartite graph may still lack a perfect matching (e.g., K_3 with k=2, odd n).

formulas.name: Tutte's Theorem (General Graphs)
formulas.latex: G \text{ has a perfect matching} \iff \forall S\subseteq V: o(G-S) \leq |S|
formulas.plain: G has a perfect matching iff for every subset S of V, the number of odd components of G-S <= |S|
formulas.whenToUse: Checking perfect matching existence in general (non-bipartite) graphs

terms.means: number of odd-order connected components of G after removing S
any subset of vertices

formulas.trap: Tutte's condition generalises Hall's; for bipartite graphs both give the same answer but Hall's is simpler.

formulas.name: Near-Perfect Matching in K_n (n odd)
formulas.latex: K_n\;(n\text{ odd}):\;\nu(K_n) = \frac{n-1}{2}
formulas.plain: Maximum matching in K_n with n odd has (n-1)/2 edges (one vertex left unmatched)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding maximum matching size in a complete graph on an odd number of vertices

terms.means: number of vertices (odd)

formulas.trap: No perfect matching exists for odd n; maximum matching leaves exactly one vertex unmatched.

formulas.name: Hopcroft-Karp Algorithm (Bipartite)
formulas.latex: \text{Time complexity: } O(\sqrt{V} \cdot E)
formulas.plain: Hopcroft-Karp bipartite matching runs in O(sqrt(V) * E) time
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about the fastest algorithm for maximum bipartite matching

terms.means: number of vertices
number of edges

formulas.trap: Augmenting-path-by-path (Hungarian BFS) runs in O(V*E); Hopcroft-Karp is faster by batching augmenting paths of the same shortest length.

formulas.name: Hungarian Algorithm (Assignment Problem)
formulas.latex: \text{Optimal assignment in } K_{n,n} \text{ with weights: } O(n^3)
formulas.plain: Hungarian algorithm solves the minimum-cost perfect matching in O(n^3)
formulas.whenToUse: When a weighted bipartite matching (assignment problem) is required

terms.means: number of workers/jobs (equal-sized bipartition)

formulas.trap: Hungarian algorithm finds min-cost perfect matching, not just any maximum matching; it is not the same as Hopcroft-Karp.
